event_name: UFC Fight Night: Muhammad vs. Bonfim

fights.fighter_a_bio: Belal Muhammad picks opponents apart with footwork and distance control, using jabs and leg kicks to set up combinations without taking big risks. He's proven at the highest level (Elo 1681), but our model gives him just a 26% edge here—he'll need to stay patient and avoid getting dragged into Bonfim's preferred range.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Gabriel Bonfim hunts takedowns aggressively and finishes from top position with ground-and-pound; he's a heavy pressure grappler who controls fights on the mat. Our model favors him at 74%—he's the clear betting pick because his wrestling typically shuts down strikers who can't defend the initial shot.
fights.watch_for: Whether Belal can use his footwork to stay at kicking range and avoid Bonfim's level-change attempts early. If Bonfim gets him down in rounds 1–2, it's likely over; if Belal keeps it standing past round 2, his Elo and experience give him a real path back.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Baraniewski enters with minimal recorded fight data, making his actual strengths and weaknesses a mystery. At Elo 1500, he's unproven at this level—our model gives him a 49.7% edge, suggesting a true toss-up.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Elekana sits nearly identical in credentials (Elo 1502), with our model giving him a razor-thin 50.3% edge. Like Baraniewski, limited fight film means this is a genuine coin flip based on what we can't see.
fights.watch_for: Watch for who dictates the pace early—whichever fighter controls center and lands first will likely carry momentum into deeper rounds since neither has a clear tactical blueprint.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Imanol Rodriguez is a slight favorite in our model (51%), operating from a near-identical experience baseline as Schnell. Without detailed fight film, he's the pick here, but this is essentially a coin flip.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Matt Schnell sits at 49% in our model—a statistical dead heat with Rodriguez. Both fighters have comparable Elo ratings, suggesting they're evenly matched on proven output.
fights.watch_for: Whoever controls the clinch and lands knees will likely win this tight matchup, as both fighters appear to have similar striking and grappling experience.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Bruno Silva lands hard and stays composed—his striking is technical enough to pick apart opponents at range, but he'll happily drag you into exchanges where his power shows up. The model favors him 66% because he's proven he can control the pace and grind out wins when things get messy.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Edgar Chairez hunts takedowns early and leans on pressure wrestling to neutralize opponents' advantages. He's got a real Elo rating edge here (1486 vs 1468), suggesting he's actually faced stiffer competition, but he'll need to execute his gameplan crisply or risk eating shots standing up.
fights.watch_for: Does Chairez secure takedowns in the first round? If he lands one clean, he controls the tempo and limits Silva's power. If Silva sprawls and stays patient on the feet, his striking advantage compounds over time.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Jeisla Chaves comes in with a slight edge in our model at 52.4%, suggesting she has the experience advantage despite similar ratings. She'll need to control the pace and avoid getting into a slugfest where Duben's power could take over.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Yuneisy Duben sits at 47.6% in our projection, making this a coin flip with marginal separation. She's the type to explode when she sees an opening, so timing and distance management will determine if she can land her shots clean.
fights.watch_for: Whether Chaves can establish a clinch game or takedown threat early—if Duben gets to trade in the pocket with space, her power advantage takes over.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Brendan Allen grinds opponents down with relentless wrestling and top control, using his strength to pin limbs and neutralize counters. He's comfortable everywhere but lethal when he dictates where the fight happens—our model gives him the edge here at 52.4%.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Edmen Shahbazyan hunts with combinations and looks to crack opponents early, mixing hands with takedown attempts to keep them honest. He's dangerous in scrambles but can get caught chasing finishes against someone willing to sit in the pocket.
fights.watch_for: Whether Allen can establish top position and hold it. If Shahbazyan keeps the fight standing and forces exchanges, his speed and power give him real chances—but Allen's wrestling control and higher Elo rating (1621 vs 1546) suggest he'll find a way to slow the pace.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Bryce Mitchell is a suffocating grappler who lives in your guard and uses top control to wear you down over 15 minutes. He's not flashy—he's patient, methodical, and built for grinding out decisions.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Victor Henry is a heavy favorite here (92% by our model) because he attacks from everywhere: he'll strike into clinch work, then drop levels for takedowns without telegraphing. He's hard to predict because he doesn't stick to one range.
fights.watch_for: Whether Henry can keep the fight standing and impose his pace, or if Mitchell successfully drags him into deep water where top control becomes Henry's problem for three rounds.

fights.fighter_a_bio: McGhee is the slight favorite here with a 56.9% model edge, backed by a higher Elo rating (1547.9) that suggests more consistent performances. He'll need to impose whatever his primary strengths are early—the chaos index of 0.5 means this fight has room to surprise.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Wiklacz comes in as the underdog at 43.1% by our model despite sitting at a respectable 1500 Elo. He's fighting an uphill battle, but equal chaos means there's a realistic path to disruption if he can exploit McGhee's tendencies.
fights.watch_for: Whether McGhee can control the fight's pace in the first two rounds—if Wiklacz survives that window without getting badly hurt, his upside improves dramatically.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Leavitt lives in the clinch and on the mat—he's always hunting takedowns and uses upper-body control to wear opponents down in tight quarters. His problem: he can get caught trading hands on the feet, and his Elo (1526.9) shows he hasn't beaten top-tier competition yet.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Brito can strike from distance, mix in takedown threats, and stay composed under pressure—our model gives him 67.6% to win because he has more tools to counter Leavitt's wrestling attack. He's slightly ahead in proven track record (Elo 1534.0) and can dictate where the fight happens.
fights.watch_for: Whether Leavitt can secure a takedown in the first two minutes and control Brito on the mat, or if Brito keeps him at range and uses footwork to stay out of clinch range long enough to land clean strikes.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Ziam mixes wrestling takedowns with clinch control to grind opponents down—he's patient and methodical, happy to work in tight and drain energy. Our model makes him a heavy 75.9% favorite because his grappling gives him multiple paths to victory.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Nolan hunts knockouts with straight hands and leg kicks from range, relying on timing and speed to land clean. He'll need to keep distance and stay sharp, but faces a 24.1% win probability against Ziam's closing style.
fights.watch_for: If Nolan lands early and keeps Ziam at distance for the first round, he can build confidence and control the pace. If Ziam closes the gap and drags him into the clinch in round one, expect Ziam to control from there and cruise to a decision.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Ketlen Souza pressures opponents with wrestling and clinch control, grinding out decisions on the scorecards. The model has her as a 73% favorite—she's the more proven fighter here with a higher Elo rating despite Carnelossi's pedigree.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Ariane Carnelossi owns a higher Elo rating and likely relies on striking volume and footwork to stay off her back. She'll need clean combinations and distance management to avoid getting dragged into Souza's grappling exchanges.
fights.watch_for: Whether Souza can land takedowns early—if she gets Carnelossi down in rounds 1–2, the wrestling control should run the clock. If Carnelossi stuffs shots and keeps it standing past round 2, her volume striking gives her a real path back into it.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Cachoeira lands hard strikes from distance and hunts takedowns when opponents back up—she's dangerous because she doesn't need to pick one approach. The model heavily favors her at 94.3%, suggesting her technical advantages are too much for this matchup.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Chandler mixes strikes with grappling attempts but hasn't shown the same precision or timing as Cachoeira at this level. She's the significant underdog (5.7% by the model) and will need perfect timing to exploit openings.
fights.watch_for: Whether Chandler can close distance and smother Cachoeira's striking before eating too many hard shots—if Cachoeira keeps range and picks Chandler apart early, this ends decisively.
